1: The 'last days' (eschatais hēmerais) characterized by moral collapse (2 Timothy 3:1-9: lovers of self, lovers of money, proud, arrogant, abusive, disobedient to parents, ungrateful, unholy, heartless, unappeasable...) represents a conventional form in Jewish and early Christian eschatological literature called the 'signs of the end' tradition — cf. 1 Enoch 91-93 (the Apocalypse of Weeks), 2 Baruch 27-30, and rabbinic descriptions of the era before Messiah's coming. Paul's use of this tradition applies it to the present crisis in Timothy's ministry, not a distant future: the last days have already begun in the false teachers' behavior.
2: 'People will be lovers of themselves (philautoi), lovers of money (philargyroi), boastful, arrogant, abusive (blasphēmoi), disobedient to parents...' — the vice list of 2 Timothy 3:2-5 is one of the NT's most extensive, with 18 terms. It belongs to the Jewish apocalyptic genre of 'signs of the end' and parallels similar lists in 1 Enoch 91-93, 2 Baruch, and the Assumption of Moses. The list's opening terms — philautoi (self-lovers) and philargyroi (money-lovers) — echo the two great loves that Jesus identified as incompatible with God-love: self and mammon (Matthew 6:24; 2 Timothy 22:37-39). The Stoic tradition also criticized philautos as the root of vice (Marcus Aurelius, Med. 9.42).
3: 'Without love (astorgoi), unforgiving (aspondoi), slanderous (diaboloi), without self-control (akrateis), brutal (anēmeroi), not lovers of good (aphilagathoi)' — the vice list continues with terms that describe the collapse of the social bonds that sustain civilization. Astorgos (without natural affection) refers specifically to the family love that even animals share; its absence signals the breakdown of the most fundamental social bond. Aspondos (unforgiving, unreconcilable) refers to refusal to enter into a truce; it describes those who cannot be pacified by any overture. Diaboloi is 'slanderers' (the same root as 'devil').
4: 'Treacherous (prodotai), reckless (propeteis), swollen with conceit (tetyphōmenoi), lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God (philēdonoi mallon ē philotheoi)' — the climactic antithesis of the vice list: philēdonos (pleasure-loving) vs. philotheos (God-loving) is the ultimate diagnostic. The philēdonos vs. philotheos antithesis is the exact reverse of what the Pastoral Epistles commend (1 Timothy 4:8: godliness holds promise for the present and the future; 2 Timothy 6:6: godliness with contentment is great gain). The Epicurean tradition's elevation of pleasure (hēdonē) as the highest good is here placed on the wrong side of the eschatological divide.
5: 'Having the appearance of godliness (morphōsin eusebeias) but denying its power (tēn de dynamin autēs ērnēmenoi)' — the counterfeit of genuine godliness: the outward form (morphōsis) without the inner reality (dynamis). This matches the profile of the false teachers throughout the Pastoral Epistles: they use the vocabulary of Christian piety but lack the Spirit-empowered character that genuine piety produces. The instruction 'avoid such people' (kai toutous apotrepou) connects the end-times vice list to the present: these people are not a future threat but the current false teachers already present in the community.
6: 'For among them are those who creep into households (endynontes eis tas oikias) and captivate weak women (gynaikaria aikhmalōtizontes)' — endyō (to slip/creep into) suggests stealthy entry rather than open teaching; the false teachers work through informal household access rather than formal assembly teaching. Gynaikaria (little women, a diminutive with pejorative connotation) describes women who are 'loaded down with sins and led astray by various passions' — the false teachers exploit spiritual vulnerability. The house-church setting made this kind of informal teaching particularly accessible: teachers moved through domestic spaces where women managed the household and received visitors.
7: 'Always learning (pantote manthanontes) and never arriving at a knowledge of the truth (epignōsin alētheias)' — the paradox of endless study without reaching the goal describes the false teachers' learning as a perpetual process with no terminus: they accumulate information without receiving transformative knowledge. The distinction between mere learning (manthanō) and knowledge (epignōsis) is qualitative, not quantitative: the knowledge of truth is a specific, personal, relational knowing of the Christ who is the truth, not an accumulation of religious information. Socrates recognized a similar paradox in the sophists who taught endlessly without producing wisdom.
8: 'Jannes and Jambres opposed Moses' — these names do not appear in the Hebrew OT; they are from Jewish oral and written tradition that identified the Egyptian magicians of Exodus 7:11-22 by name. They appear in the Damascus Document (CD 5.17-19) from Qumran, in Origen, Pliny the Elder, and in various pseudepigraphical works. Paul's citation of this extra-biblical tradition demonstrates his engagement with Jewish interpretive tradition; the tradition was apparently well-known enough to assume Timothy's familiarity. The magicians who counterfeited Moses's miracles are the type of the false teachers who counterfeit the gospel.
9: 'But they will not get very far, because their folly (anoia) will be plain to all' — the 'folly' of the false teachers is the same term used of the magicians' failure: just as Jannes and Jambres were eventually exposed when they could not match Moses's signs (Exodus 8:18-19), the false teachers will be exposed when their counterfeit spiritual reality is revealed. The confidence Paul expresses is not naive; it is grounded in the precedent that God's truth ultimately exposes its imitators, however impressive their initial appearance.
10: 'But you have followed (parēkolouthēsas) my teaching, conduct, purpose, faith, patience, love, steadfastness' — parakolouthēo means to follow closely, to trace the course of; Timothy has tracked Paul's entire life and ministry as an apprentice tracks a master. The nine-item list of Paul's apostolic character (teaching, conduct, purpose, faith, patience, love, steadfastness, persecutions, sufferings) is the Pauline periautologia (self-commendation) that 2 Corinthians makes explicit (2 Cor 11-12): the apostle's life is the curriculum, not only his doctrine.
11: 'What happened to me at Antioch, at Iconium, and at Lystra' — the three cities of Paul's first missionary journey (Acts 13:14-14:20), all in the Roman province of Galatia. Antioch Pisidia: Paul expelled from the city (Acts 13:50). Iconium: plot to stone him (Acts 14:5). Lystra: actually stoned and left for dead (Acts 14:19-20). Timothy was from Lystra (Acts 16:1) and likely witnessed, or heard eyewitness accounts of, the stoning. The appeal to events Timothy personally knows is the most direct argument for perseverance: the apostle you know survived all of this; you can endure your current difficulties too.
12: 'All who desire to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted' (diōchthēsontai) — the universal claim is supported by Paul's experience and by Jesus's teaching (John 15:20: if they persecuted me, they will persecute you). The Greco-Roman world's hostility to the exclusive truth-claims of the gospel was predictable: a community that refused to participate in the imperial cult, refused to eat at pagan temples, and proclaimed a crucified criminal as the world's true Lord was culturally disruptive. The sociological pressure on the early churches was real and is here treated as the normal rather than the exceptional pattern.
13: 'Evildoers and imposters (goētes) will go from bad to worse, deceiving and being deceived' — goēs was the term for a charlatan, a sorcerer who used tricks and incantations to deceive; in Hellenistic rhetoric it described the lowest type of pseudo-intellectual, contrasted with the genuine philosopher. The self-deception of the false teachers — they deceive others while being themselves deceived — is the standard feature of the apocalyptic description of the last days' moral collapse: the deceiver is also deceived by the same powers that work through him.
14: 'Continue (mene) in what you have learned and have firmly believed, knowing from whom you have learned it' — the stability commanded (menō, to remain, abide — the same verb in John 15's vine-and-branches passage) contrasts with the false teachers' drift. The 'from whom' (para tinos) — plural — identifies both Paul (the apostolic tradition) and the family (Lois and Eunice, 2 Timothy 1:5): Timothy's formation has a double source, apostolic and domestic. The Pastoral Epistles' insistence on the personal identity of the teacher as the guarantee of the teaching reflects a pre-textual world where oral tradition carried authority through trusted persons.
15: 'From childhood (apo brepheus) you have known the sacred writings (hiera grammata)' — apo brepheus means from infancy, from the very youngest age; hieros (sacred/holy) + grammata (letters, writings) is the term for the Hebrew Scriptures, used in Hellenistic Jewish literature (Josephus, Ant. 20.264: from earliest youth he studied the sacred writings; Philo). The reference to the holy Scriptures that Timothy knew from infancy confirms the Jewish family environment (Lois and Eunice) in which the Scriptures were central to formation. These sacred writings are able 'to make wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus' — a Christological hermeneutic: the OT's wisdom is salvation-directed and Christ-oriented.
16: 'All Scripture (pāsa graphē) is breathed out by God (theopneustos)' — theopneustos (God-breathed) is a Pauline coinage (hapax legomenon), combining theos (God) and pneō (breathe); the compound means 'breathed out by God' rather than 'breathed into' — Scripture is the product of the divine breath, not the recipient of it. The OT analogy is Genesis 2:7 (YHWH breathed into Adam's nostrils) and the Spirit-wind of Ezek 37; both suggest a life-giving, animating breath. The referent is primarily the OT (what Timothy knew from infancy, 2 Timothy 3:15), though 2 Peter 3:16 treats Paul's letters as Scripture, implying the principle's eventual extension.
17: 'That the person of God may be complete (artios), equipped (exērtismenos) for every good work' — artios (complete, perfectly fitted for a purpose) and exērtismenos (fully equipped, perfect participle) together describe the state of the one who has been thoroughly formed by Scripture: not merely knowledgeable but prepared-for-action in every conceivable situation. The goal of scriptural formation is practical: 'every good work' (pan ergon agathon). The Pastoral Epistles' insistence on the coupling of right doctrine and good works (Titus 3:8; 2 Timothy 2:14) finds its Scriptural grounding here: Scripture produces both the correct understanding and the equipped character.
